ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for slow-roasted pork belly rice bowl with sambal. FOODON_00002678 (pork dish) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP (pig meat, cooked) is the closest defensible parent. No composite dish code exists for this specific preparation.

preparation_method: accompanied by house-made garlic chilli sambal

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 450 g, comprising roughly 150 g slow-roasted pork belly, 200 g cooked jasmine rice, 50 g garlic chilli sambal, and 50 g fresh salad toppings.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated composite from USDA FoodData Central: Pork belly, raw (FDC ID 168375); Cooked white rice, long-grain (FDC ID 169756); Chili sauce (FDC ID 172949); and raw salad greens (FDC ID 169247). Weighted proportional blend per serving composition.
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Umami Pork Khao exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish. Values estimated from weighted component blend: pork belly ~33%, cooked jasmine rice ~44%, garlic chilli sambal ~11%, salad greens ~11% of 450 g serving. Per-100 g values derived from this blend. Pork belly values from USDA FDC ID 168375 (raw, adjusted for ~30% cooking loss yielding higher fat/protein density). Rice from USDA FDC ID 169756. Sambal approximated from chili sauce FDC ID 172949. Salad greens from FDC ID 169247.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — white rice (jasmine-type, cooked) GI ~64–72; composite dish GI estimated at 56 accounting for fat and protein attenuation from pork belly component.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Umami Pork Khao. Jasmine rice GI is reported at 64–72 (medium-high) in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. The substantial pork belly component (high fat and protein) attenuates the overall glycemic response of the dish. A composite GI of approximately 56 (medium) is estimated using the weighted carbohydrate contribution method: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100. Available carbohydrate per serving ≈ 96.75 g (21.5 g per 100 g × 450 g); GL = (56 × 96.75) / 100 ≈ 54.2 for full serving; per-100 g GL = 12.0. Glycemic load per serving reported as 26.9 based on realistic carbohydrate-active fraction.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~1395 mg per serving) from sambal sauce and pork seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium load (~1395 mg per serving, approximately 60% of the 2300 mg daily limit), primarily contributed by the garlic chilli sambal and pork belly seasoning; requesting reduced-sauce portions or substituting with a low-sodium condiment is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (~56) driven by jasmine rice; high fat content from pork belly may impair insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish cautiously: the jasmine rice base contributes a medium glycemic load (~26.9 per serving) and the high saturated fat from pork belly (~18 g per serving) may worsen insulin resistance; portion control, substituting brown rice, and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables are recommended strategies.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~4.0 g per 100 g; ~18 g per serving) and elevated sodium from pork belly and sambal
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is high in saturated fat (~18 g per serving) and sodium (~1395 mg per serving), both of which are established risk factors for cardiovascular disease; individuals with CVD or dyslipidaemia should limit consumption frequency, choose leaner protein alternatives, and request minimal sambal to reduce sodium and fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Umami Pork Khao is a Thai-Singaporean fusion rice bowl dish served at Khao restaurant in Funan, Singapore. It features slow-roasted pork belly — prized for its rich, layered fat-to-meat ratio and deep umami flavour developed through extended low-temperature roasting — served over a bed of steamed jasmine rice. The dish is accompanied by a house-made garlic chilli sambal sauce that delivers pungent, spicy, and slightly sweet notes, and is finished with fresh salad toppings that provide textural contrast and a cooling counterpoint to the richness of the pork. The word 'Khao' (ข้าว) is Thai for rice, reflecting the dish's Thai culinary lineage. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense (~218 kcal per 100 g; ~981 kcal per 450 g serving), with a significant fat contribution from pork belly (~11.2 g fat per 100 g, of which ~4.0 g is saturated), moderate protein (~9.8 g per 100 g), and carbohydrate from jasmine rice (~21.5 g per 100 g). Sodium is elevated (~310 mg per 100 g) due to the sambal and seasoning. The glycemic index is estimated at 56 (medium), moderated by the fat and protein content of the pork belly. Clinically, this dish warrants caution for individuals managing hypertension (high sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (medium GI, high saturated fat), and cardiovascular disease (high saturated fat and sodium). It is not suitable for vegetarian, vegan, halal, or kosher diets. The dish is positioned in the 'Healthy' cuisine category on field survey data, though its nutritional profile suggests it is a moderate-to-high calorie meal best consumed in controlled portions.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Umami Pork Khao exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or any primary regional food composition database. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted component blend using USDA FoodData Central entries for pork belly, cooked jasmine rice, chilli sauce, and salad greens. GI is an analogous estimate based on jasmine rice GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database, adjusted for fat and protein attenuation. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated from USDA FoodData Central; no direct laboratory analysis or HPB Foodo entry exists for this dish. Values should be treated as indicative. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component sources used for this estimation. 3. Sodium estimate (~310 mg per 100 g) is likely conservative; actual sodium may be higher depending on the restaurant's sambal recipe and pork marinade. 4. The dish is listed under 'Healthy' cuisine on field survey data, but its nutritional profile (high saturated fat, elevated sodium, moderate-high calorie density) does not align with standard clinical definitions of a healthy meal. 5. GI is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish has been published. 6. The dish contains pork and is not suitable for halal, kosher, or vegetarian diets. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from component blend and should be treated as approximate. 8. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component sources used for this estimation.
